coredata.dc:title: Boosting photocatalytic efficiency through advanced hollow structure GQDs@ZIS@HCOF composites
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Mohammadi Ziarani, Ghodsi
Banitalebi, Ali
Badiei, Alireza
coredata.dc:description: The development of highly efficient photocatalysts is crucial for addressing environmental pollution. In this study, we present a novel ternary hierarchical hollow structure composed of ZnIn2S4 nanoplates grown on a hollow covalent organic framework (HCOF) and functionalized with graphene quantum dots (GQDs), synthesized via a templating method followed by a solvothermal process. This innovative GQDs@ZIS@HCOF composite exhibits exceptional photocatalytic performance for ciprofloxacin degradation under visible light irradiation. The optimized photocatalyst incorporating 3 wt% GQDs demonstrates a significant degradation efficiency, achieving 90 % degradation within a 120-minute duration. This performance surpasses that of pure HCOF and ZnIn2S4 by factors of six and seven, respectively. The superior activity is attributed to (i) the hierarchical hollow structure, which enhances light harvesting and provides abundant active sites, and (ii) the synergistic interactions among the components, which facilitate efficient charge separation and transfer, thereby extending the photo-response range. This work not only demonstrates a significant enhancement in photocatalytic efficiency but also offers a strategic design for next-generation three-dimensional photocatalysts with broad applications in environmental remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Covalent organic frameworks
Hollow structure
ZnIn2S4
Graphene quantum dots
Visible-light photocatalysis

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the Alzahra University and the University of Tehran for assisting this research financially.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram illustrating the creation of the GQDs@ZIS@HCOF heterojunction.
FESEM (a-d) and TEM (e, f) visuals of the hollow structure photocatalysts at each stage of the fabrication process.

(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms for HCOF, ZIS, and GQDs@ZIS@HCOF; (b) UV–vis absorbance spectra for the prepared samples; and (c) Powder XRD patterns for pure HCOF, ZIS, ZIS@HCOF, and the ternary composite.
(a) Photoluminescence spectra of the samples at room temperature, (b) transient photocurrent responses, and (c) EIS changes for the HCOF, ZIS, and GQDs@ZIS@HCOF samples.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation efficiency of ciprofloxacin under visible light over pure HCOF, pure ZIS, and composite samples, (b and c) kinetic curves and rate constants for ciprofloxacin degradation with the photocatalysts.

a) Impact of various scavengers (IPA, BQ, and EDTA) on the photodegradation of ciprofloxacin using the GQDs@ZIS@HCOF photocatalyst, b) emission intensity of TAOH upon irradiating a basic TA solution over GQDs@ZIS@HCOF at λ ex of 315 nm with varying irradiation durations.
Diagram illustrating the potential photocatalytic mechanism for the degradation of ciprofloxacin using the ternary composite.
(a) Photocatalytic stability tests of GQDs@ZIS@HCOF for ciprofloxacin degradation across four cycles; (b) XRD patterns and SEM images of GQDs@ZIS@HCOF before and after the photocatalytic reaction.

head.ce:title: Boosting photocatalytic efficiency through advanced hollow structure GQDs@ZIS@HCOF composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Alzahra University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Organic Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Alzahra University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Organic Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Alzahra University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of highly efficient photocatalysts is crucial for addressing environmental pollution. In this study, we present a novel ternary hierarchical hollow structure composed of ZnIn2S4 nanoplates grown on a hollow covalent organic framework (HCOF) and functionalized with graphene quantum dots (GQDs), synthesized via a templating method followed by a solvothermal process. This innovative GQDs@ZIS@HCOF composite exhibits exceptional photocatalytic performance for ciprofloxacin degradation under visible light irradiation. The optimized photocatalyst incorporating 3 wt% GQDs demonstrates a significant degradation efficiency, achieving 90 % degradation within a 120-minute duration. This performance surpasses that of pure HCOF and ZnIn2S4 by factors of six and seven, respectively. The superior activity is attributed to (i) the hierarchical hollow structure, which enhances light harvesting and provides abundant active sites, and (ii) the synergistic interactions among the components, which facilitate efficient charge separation and transfer, thereby extending the photo-response range. This work not only demonstrates a significant enhancement in photocatalytic efficiency but also offers a strategic design for next-generation three-dimensional photocatalysts with broad applications in environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request. The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: The challenges of energy shortages, environmental pollution, and effluent treatment are among the most pressing issues in contemporary technology and science [1,2]. Consequently, there is an urgent need for the development of advanced materials and renewable energy technologies to address these critical challenges [3]. Semiconductor-based photocatalysis represents an environmentally friendly method for the rapid decomposition of organic pollutants under mild conditions, yielding more eco-friendly byproducts such as H2O and CO2 [4,5]. In the synthesis of semiconductor materials, three essential factors must be considered: (1) light absorption capacity, (2) specific surface area, and (3) the separation and migration efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Semiconductor materials with appropriate band gaps have the potential to enhance the rate of specific chemical reactions under light illumination [6,7]. To date, various semiconductor photocatalysts with suitable band gap structures have been developed, including metal oxides, phosphides, sulfides, and metal-free polymers [8,9].

Covalent organic frameworks (COFs) are crystalline organic porous polymers that are interconnected by covalent bonds formed from organic monomers [10,11]. Due to their characteristics, including robust π-π interactions between adjacent layers and extensive conjugated systems within those layers, COFs exhibit broad absorption spectra and a pronounced capacity to capture visible light [12,13]. As a result, they have garnered significant interest in various applications, including photocatalytic water splitting, carbon dioxide reduction, pollution degradation, and organic transformations [14,15]. Most pure COFs demonstrate relatively low photocatalytic efficiency, primarily due to the high recombination rate of photogenerated charge carriers and low light harvesting efficiency [16,17]. Consequently, a variety of efforts have been undertaken to address these limitations, including the incorporation of metal or nonmetal dopants, the regulation of morphological characteristics, the engineering of textural properties, the coupling with other semiconductor materials, as well as surface modification techniques and the integration with carbonaceous substances [18–20].

One of the most effective strategies for enhancing charge separation efficiency is the combination of COFs with other semiconductors to form heterostructure materials [21–23]. This approach has been substantiated across various photocatalytic systems. Recent advancements in research have increasingly highlighted metal sulfides as inorganic semiconductors, particularly due to their unique electronic and optical properties that lend themselves to photocatalytic applications [24,25]. Among these materials, layered ZnIn2S4 (ZIS), characterized by an alternating overlapping structure of S-Zn-S-In-S-In-S along the Z-axis, has emerged as a promising photocatalyst [26]. The attributes of ZIS, including its high activity, ease of synthesis, appropriate band gap for visible light absorption, and commendable chemical stability, render it an appealing candidate for the development of highly active heterojunctions when integrated with COFs [27,28].

Morphology regulation has widely been employed to enhance the photocatalytic activity of photocatalysts [29,30]. A notable structure that imitates the thylakoid, which is integral to photosynthesis in nature, is the hollow sphere. This design leverages a series of physicochemical processes occurring on both sides of its membrane, effectively converting solar energy and storing it in chemical form [31–33]. Recently, there has been a significant increase in scientific interest regarding the fabrication of hollow microspheres and nanospheres due to their unique physicochemical properties and potential functional applications, including gas sensing, energy storage, catalysis, and drug delivery [33]. In particular, hollow-structured materials, characterized by their inner cavities and thin porous shells, promote the efficient mass transfer of guest molecules and facilitate the migration of photogenerated charges [34,35]. This leads to enhanced charge collection and separation at the interface, thereby providing distinct advantages for solar energy conversion in photochemical reactions [36]. Consequently, researchers have focused considerable attention on the following advantages of hollow structures. First, the presence of an internal cavity enhances light absorption through multiple reflections and the slow-photon effect. Second, such structures provide an increased number of active sites compared to their bulk counterparts. Lastly, thin shells minimize the likelihood of photogenerated electrons being annihilated during their transit [37–39].

Hierarchical hollow heterostructures comprising two-dimensional nanosheets are extensively employed for the conversion of solar energy and its storage in chemical form, attributable to their intrinsic structural advantages [40]. Specifically, hierarchical materials possess interconnected porous networks that facilitate the transportation of guest species, thereby enhancing reaction dynamics [41,42]. Furthermore, hollow scaffolds featuring interior cavities are capable of trapping light, an effect that is amplified in multi-shelled structures. Nanosheet-based hollow architectures also exhibit a reduced charge diffusion pathway, an increased surface area, and a higher concentration of active sites [43]. More importantly, this architecture establishes a strong interface between the two semiconductor components, which is advantageous for enhanced charge separation and improved photocatalytic performance. These characteristics significantly mitigate the recombination of electron-hole pairs, augment the capture and adsorption of analytes, and enhance the kinetics of photo-redox reactions compared to conventional hollow structures [44].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) represent a novel class of carbon nanomaterials characterized by their high electron conductivity and electron reservoir capabilities. These properties enable GQDs to function effectively as electron mediators between two semiconductors, thereby reducing the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs. In the context of photocatalysis, high-conductivity GQDs are typically employed as mediators for charge storage and as electron acceptors, facilitating enhanced charge separation [45,46]. Numerous studies have demonstrated that heterogeneous catalysts incorporating GQDs exhibit significant photocatalytic activity, highlighting their advantageous characteristics in this field [47–49].

Based on these considerations, we have designed an innovative core-shell structure utilizing hollow triazine-based COF as the polymeric scaffold and ZIS flakes as the shell which was further modified with GQDs for application in visible-light photocatalysis. Our approach entails three key aspects. First, the unique morphology of the photocatalyst provides a shorter charge diffusion pathway and facilitates multiple light reflections within the cavity, thereby enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. Second, the intimate junction between the ZIS shell and the COF core in this architecture promotes the effective separation of charge carriers. Lastly, the synergistic interaction between ZIS, COF, and GQDs reduces the recombination rate of charge carriers while increasing light absorption capacity. The photocatalytic activity of this engineered structure was evaluated through the degradation of ciprofloxacin, an antibiotic pollutant, under visible light irradiation. This study paves the way for the design of tailored nanostructures aimed at advanced photochemical applications.
The materials, instrumentation, and electrochemical assessments utilized in this research are detailed in the Supplementary Information.

A monodisperse spherical silica template was synthesized following a modified version of a previously established procedure [50]. The synthesis commenced with the preparation of a solution consisting of 8 mL of aqueous ammonia (32 wt%) and 10 mL of water, which was subsequently added to 160 mL of ethanol. This mixture was stirred for 20 minutes at a temperature of 35 °C. Following this, 8 mL of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) was incorporated into the solution while maintaining vigorous stirring. The resultant mixture was allowed to stand undisturbed for 1 hour, facilitating the formation of monodisperse solid silica spheres. The products were isolated through high-speed centrifugation and underwent multiple washing steps with ethanol to ensure purity. To modify the surface of SiO2 nanoparticles with amino groups, a comprehensive dispersion of 1 g of previously synthesized SiO2 nanoparticles in 100 mL of anhydrous toluene was accomplished through a 30-minute sonication procedure. Following this, 1 mL of (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) and 2 mL of toluene were added to the dispersion. The mixture was then refluxed under an N2 atmosphere for a period of 24 hours. To remove any unbound NH2 groups from the resultant particles, centrifugation and several washes with ethanol were carried out. Finally, the particles were dried at 70 °C for 24 hours.

To initiate the nucleation of seeds, a solution was prepared by dissolving 1,4-phenylenediamine (PDA, 0.048 mmol) and tri-(4-formacylphenoxy)-1,3,5-triazine (TFPTA, 0.016 mmol) in a 5 mL suspension of amine-functionalized SiO2 nanoparticles. This suspension, which consisted of ortho-dichlorobenzene (O-DCB) and n-butanol (n-But) in a volumetric ratio of 4:1, served as the solvent medium. Continuous stirring of the mixture was conducted for a duration of 2 hours. Following this initial period, stirring was extended for an additional hour after the incorporation of 10 μL of acetic acid. In the subsequent step, PDA (0.3 mmol) and TFPTA (0.082 mmol) were dissolved in the previously prepared seed solution, utilizing sonication for 15 minutes. Subsequently, 0.3 mL of acetic acid was added to the solution. The reaction mixture was promptly frozen in a liquid nitrogen bath at 77 K, followed by degassing through three cycles of freeze-pump-thaw, after which it was sealed under vacuum and heated at 120 °C for 3 days. The isolation of the resultant product was accomplished through centrifugation and followed by sequential washing with THF, anhydrous ethanol, and acetone. The obtained precipitate was subsequently dried in an oven under vacuum conditions at 80°C for 12 hours. The dissolution of the silica framework was performed using an aqueous solution of NH4HF2 at 70°C for 16 hours. The final product was collected via centrifugation, underwent multiple washes with deionized water, and was subsequently dried under a vacuum.

The fabrication of ZnIn2S4 nanosheets on the surface of HCOF was accomplished by employing a solvothermal technique. A 100 mL round-bottom flask was utilized to combine 25 mL of water (adjusted with 0.5 M HCl) and 10 mL of glycerol under stirring, resulting in a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, 272 mg of ZnCl2, 586 mg of InCl3·4H2O, and 300 mg of thioacetamide were dissolved in the aforementioned solution using ultrasonication. Following this, 56 mg of HCOF was introduced into the solution, and the resulting mixture was agitated for 40 minutes to facilitate the adsorption process. The mixture was then transferred to an oil bath maintained at a temperature of 85 °C, where it was continuously agitated for 2 hours. The final product was obtained through centrifugation, followed by multiple washes with ethanol, and was subsequently dried at 50 °C under vacuum conditions. Additionally, the synthesis of pure ZnIn2S4 was conducted under identical experimental conditions without the inclusion of a substrate.

Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from purified graphite powder utilizing a modified Hummers method [51]. The preparation of GOQDs followed an established protocol. In a standard procedure, GO was dispersed in dimethylformamide (DMF) at a concentration of 300 mg per 10 mL. The GO/DMF solution underwent ultrasonication for a duration of 30 minutes. Following this, the mixed solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, ensuring a filling ratio of 80 %. The sealed autoclave was maintained at a temperature of 220 °C for a period of 8 hours. The resultant product comprised a brown transparent suspension and black precipitates. The brown transparent suspension was subsequently subjected to vacuum evaporation at 80 °C to yield solid GOQDs, which were then redissolved in water.

A total of 100 mg of ZIS@HCOF and the specified quantities of GQDs (1, 2, 3, and 4 wt% relative to the weight of ZIS@HCOF) were dispersed in 20 mL of ethanol solution using a bath sonicator for 15 minutes. Subsequently, the entire mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined steel autoclave and heated to 100 °C for 12 hours. Following this, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally, and the sample was carefully extracted via centrifugation, rinsed with ethanol and water multiple times, and then dried overnight at 70 °C.

The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized samples was assessed by measuring the degradation of ciprofloxacin under simulated solar light from a 300 W Xe lamp equipped with a 400 nm cutoff filter. In a standard photodegradation experiment, 100 mg of photocatalyst was mixed with 100 mL of a 25 ppm ciprofloxacin aqueous solution and stirred in the dark for 20 minutes to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, the suspension was exposed to irradiation from the 300 W Xe lamp while maintaining the temperature at room temperature using a circulating water system. At predetermined intervals, 4 mL of the reaction mixture was extracted and centrifuged to remove the photocatalyst particles. The concentrations of ciprofloxacin were analyzed using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Raleigh UV-1600) at a wavelength of 277 nm.

The method used was comparable to that of photocatalytic degradation, but it involved replacing the organic pollutants with terephthalic acid (TA) at an initial concentration of 5 × 10−4 mol L−1 in a dilute sodium hydroxide solution (2 × 10−3 mol L−1). At specified time intervals, a 4 mL sample of the reaction mixture was taken and centrifuged to separate the catalyst particles. Subsequently, the fluorescence emission was recorded at 425 nm, with an excitation wavelength set at 315 nm.

The hollow core-shell nano-photoreactor was synthesized utilizing a hard template strategy in conjunction with a solvothermal process, as illustrated in Fig. 1. Initially, uniform silica nanospheres modified with amine groups were produced to serve as sacrificial templates for the formation of the final structure. Organic building blocks were subsequently deposited onto the surface of the amine-functionalized silica nanospheres. Upon completion of this step, the silica cores were eliminated by dissolution in an NH4HF2 solution, resulting in the production of hollow COF spheres. Following the removal of the silica template, hydrothermal decoration of ZIS on the surface of the HCOF was conducted to achieve the core-shell architecture. Lastly, the decoration of GQDs on the surface of the ZIS@HCOF was conducted to achieve the final architecture.

Fig. 2(a-d) present FESEM images corresponding to each stage of the fabrication process. Fig. 2a distinctly illustrates that the synthesized silica templates exhibit a uniform structure, characterized by an average diameter of 150 nanometers. Following the polycondensation of the organic building blocks, SiO2@COF particles were derived, possessing a monodispersed spherical nanostructure with an average diameter of 210 nanometers. Fig. 2b and e present the SEM and TEM images of the HCOF subsequent to the removal of the silica template, respectively. The TEM image substantiates the presence of a hollow interior within the COF, with the core cavity size estimated to be approximately 150 nanometers, which corresponds to the dimensions of the silica nanosphere templates. The FESEM and TEM images of ZIS@HCOF are illustrated in Fig. 2c and f, respectively. The FESEM image indicates that the ZIS nanoplates grow consistently on the surface of the COF. Furthermore, the TEM images confirm that the hollow core-shell structure comprises a hollow COF material encased by an ultrathin ZIS shell. Upon the hydrothermal decoration of GQDs on the surface of ZIS@HCOF, it is observed that the GQDs@ZIS@HCOF structures predominantly preserve the spherical morphology characteristic of core-shell particles (Fig. 2d). However, the GQDs were not distinctly visible in the FESEM images due to their diminutive size, low concentration, and the interference from the ZIS plates. The TEM image in Fig. 2g verifies that the GQDs, were effectively loaded onto the surface of the core-shell particles, as marked by yellow arrows.

The porosity and surface area of the catalyst significantly influence its photocatalytic efficiency. An increased specific surface area results in a greater number of active sites, thereby enhancing photocatalytic performance. In order to characterize the detailed porous structure of the synthesized samples, N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were conducted ( Fig. 3a). The textural properties of the samples, which include specific surface areas, total pore volumes, and average pore sizes, are presented in Table S1. The N2 sorption isotherm of the HCOF exhibits a type IV classification, indicating a significant presence of mesopores within the synthesized hollow nanospheres. Notably, the isotherm demonstrates a clear capillary condensation step at relative pressures (P/P0) ranging from 0.2 to 0.3, which suggests a considerable quantity of mesopores characterized by a narrow size distribution in the COF shells following the removal of the silica template from the COF-silica composite. The HCOF exhibits permanent microporosity, with a surface area of 1846 m²/g, which ranks among the highest available for two-dimensional COF materials. This surface area even surpasses that of the recently reported HCOF synthesized through self-template and hard template methodologies, thereby positioning it as a promising candidate for efficient photocatalytic applications. Furthermore, an evident hysteresis loop appears within the high-pressure region, indicating that the pores in the COF shell are directly connected to the hollow interior. This observation is consistent with TEM results. However, the hysteresis loop aligns more closely with the H4 type, corroborating findings related to hollow spheres composed of silica templates as reported by Kooyman et al. [52]. The elevated surface area of the HCOF, in comparison to its shapeless synthesis, can be attributed to the availability of accessible pores on both the interior and exterior surfaces, as well as the presence of a hollow interior space. Nonetheless, following the incorporation of ZIS plates and GQDs around the HCOF, both the BET surface area and pore volume exhibited a reduction; however, these values remain sufficiently high to ensure favorable properties for photocatalytic reactions.

The ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption characteristics of the synthesized samples are illustrated in Fig. 3b. It is evident that the pure ZIS demonstrates a relatively low absorption intensity, attributed to intrinsic electronic transitions. Conversely, the HCOF exhibits a robust absorption intensity across both the ultraviolet and visible regions, indicating that the HCOF material possesses a wide range of visible light absorption. The absorption intensities of the ZIS@HCOF heterojunction sample are positioned between those of HCOF and ZnIn2S4. In comparison to ZIS@HCOF, the GQD@ZIS@HCOF sample demonstrates an enhancement in visible-light absorption. This finding confirms that the GQDs effectively integrate onto the surface of the core-shell nanoparticles, thereby altering the optical properties of the resultant photocatalyst. Furthermore, the band gap energy (Eg) for both the HCOF and ZnIn2S4 was determined using Tauc's equation. As shown in Figure S1, the estimated Eg values for HCOF and ZIS are 1.97 eV and 2.26 eV, respectively.

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) measurements have confirmed that the HCOF exhibits a crystalline structure characterized by three prominent peaks at 4.70, 5.43, and 7.19 degrees, corresponding to reflections from the (110), (200), and (120) facets, respectively (see Fig. 3c). This finding indicates that the removal of the silica core did not compromise the crystalline integrity of HCOF. Furthermore, the synthesized ZnIn2S4 displays high-intensity diffraction peaks at 21.5, 27.6, 47.2, and 52.4 degrees, which correspond to the (006), (102), (104), and (110) crystal planes, respectively, aligning with the standards established in PDF#65–2023 for hexagonal ZnIn2S4. The GQDs@ZIS@HCOF heterostructure samples demonstrate a slight diffraction peak corresponding to the (100) crystal plane attributed to HCOF, in addition to the diffraction peaks associated with the hexagonal phase of ZnIn2S4. This observation indicates the simultaneous existence of both HCOF and ZIS phases within the ZIS@HCOF heterostructure. Furthermore, no diffraction peak corresponding to GQDs was observed in the GQDs@ZIS@HCOF sample. This absence is likely attributable to the limited crystallinity and minimal concentration of GQDs within the composite.

Under standard conditions, semiconductor materials can generate electron-hole pairs upon irradiation, with these photoinduced charge carriers participating in the photocatalytic process. The photoactivity of semiconductor catalysts is largely determined by the dynamics of separation and recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Photoluminescence emission spectra were utilized to assess the charge transfer and recombination behaviors of charge carriers within the photocatalysts. It is widely accepted that a low photoluminescence (PL) intensity correlates with a diminished recombination rate of the photogenerated charge carriers. Fig. 4a presents a comparison of the PL emission spectra for the synthesized samples. The PL spectrum of pure COF reveals an emission peak centered at 495 nm. A notable reduction in PL intensity is observed in the GQDs@ZIS@HCOF composites, suggesting that the formation of a heterostructure effectively suppresses the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs. This finding indicates that the establishment of the heterostructure is essential for enhancing charge separation efficiency. Additionally, the amount of GQDs affects the intensity of the PL spectrum, demonstrating the effective role of GQDs in mitigating the recombination of photo-generated charge carriers. To investigate the dynamics process of charge separation, we also performed time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectroscopy (Figure S2). The average lifetimes of HCOF, ZIS@HCOF, and GQDs@ZIS@HCOF were 4.78 ns, 2.25 ns and 1.62 ns, respectively. Compared with HCOF and ZIS@HCOF, the fluorescence lifetimes of GQDs@ZIS@HCOF heterojunction were shortened. The shortening of charge life was connected to the promotion of charge transfer. The transient photocurrent response was simultaneously evaluated to investigate the charge transfer behavior of the catalysts. It was observed that all samples exhibited repeatable and stable photocurrent profiles under light illumination throughout successive on/off cycles. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the GQDs@ZIS@HCOF (3 wt%) electrodes demonstrated the highest photocurrent density. This finding further substantiates that the ternary samples possess superior efficiency in charge separation and transfer. Correspondingly, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements presented in Fig. 4c reinforce these conclusions. In comparison to the pure sample, the ternary photocatalysts exhibited a smaller arc radius, indicating enhanced electrical conductivity. The consistency of these analytical results confirms that the formation of a heterojunction between ZIS and HCOF, as well as the incorporation of GQDs, contributes significantly to the generation of more effective charge carriers during the photocatalytic process.

The impact of GQDs content on the photocatalytic performance of the GQDs@ZIS@HCOF composite was systematically evaluated through the degradation of ciprofloxacin under visible light conditions. The results of a control experiment indicated that the degradation of ciprofloxacin without the addition of any photocatalyst was negligible, thereby underscoring the necessity of a photocatalyst for effective degradation. As illustrated in Fig. 5a, it is evident that all core-shell samples exhibit superior photocatalytic performance compared to pure COF and ZIS plates when subjected to visible light irradiation. Although the modification of the hollow spherical COF with a ZnIn2S4 layer and GQDs results in a reduced specific surface area, the photocatalytic activity is significantly enhanced. This improvement is attributed to the synergistic effects among the components: the ZnIn2S4 layer extends the light absorption range and provides favorable band alignment for efficient electron transfer, while the graphene quantum dots facilitate rapid charge separation by acting as electron mediators. Consequently, the remaining surface area is still sufficient for effective reactant adsorption, and the overall charge transport and light-harvesting efficiencies are markedly improved.

Additionally, the quantity of GQDs played a critical role in the ciprofloxacin photodegradation process over the GQDs@ZIS@HCOF composite. As the GQDs concentration increased from 1 to 3 wt%, the removal efficiency of the composite rose from 62 % to 90 % within a 120-minute period. However, it was observed that further increasing the GQDs content to 4 wt% resulted in a distinct decline in photocatalytic efficiency. This phenomenon may be attributed to the fact that a suitable quantity of GQDs may function as electron traps that enhance the separation of charge carriers, while excessive GQDs may compete for the photons essential for the semiconductors, thus reducing the number of active photocatalytic sites [53,54]. Furthermore, the performance data of the catalyst (GQDs@ZIS@HCOF) is comparable to that of other photocatalysts documented in the literature for the degradation of ciprofloxacin, as detailed in Table S2.

To gain deeper insights into the degradation dynamics of ciprofloxacin across various samples, the experimental results were analyzed using a pseudo-first-order kinetic model represented by the following equation (Eq. 1): ln(Ct/C0) = kt (1) where Ct and C0 denote the concentration at irradiation time t (in minutes) and the initial concentration of ciprofloxacin, respectively, and k represents the reaction rate constant (Fig. 5b). As evidenced in Fig. 5c, the GQDs@ZIS@HCOF composite (at 3 wt%) exhibited the highest rate constant among all photocatalysts, being 7, 6, and 3 times greater than that of pure ZIS, HCOF, and ZIS@HCOF, respectively. These results further highlight that the formation of a heterojunction between HCOF and ZIS nanoplates, in conjunction with the modification of the structure by GQDs, facilitates rapid charge carrier transfer. The aforementioned outcomes are consistent with prior characterization results, confirming that GQDs@ZIS@HCOF represents a promising candidate for photocatalytic applications driven by visible light. However, in the charge suppression experiments, it was observed that the sample containing 4 wt% GQDs exhibited marginally superior performance compared to the sample with 3 wt%. However, in the experiments focused on charge suppression, it was noted that the sample containing 4 wt% GQD demonstrated slightly enhanced performance relative to the sample comprising 3 wt%. However, the findings from the photocatalytic experiments revealed that the sample with 3 wt% GQDs achieved the highest performance levels. This result can be attributed to the excessive presence of GQDs, which may compete for photons that are critical for the action of the semiconductors. This competition reduces the availability of active photocatalytic sites, thereby making the 3 wt% sample the optimal choice across all experiments conducted.

To gain a comprehensive understanding of the photocatalytic reaction mechanism involved in the degradation of ciprofloxacin, a series of radical scavenging experiments were conducted employing the GQDs@ZIS@HCOF photocatalyst. The methodology for these trapping experiments was consistent with the protocols established for the photocatalytic degradation tests, with the exception of the incorporation of specific scavengers into the reaction medium. Three primary scavengers were utilized: 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ, 0.05 mM), disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA-2Na, 1.3 mM), and isopropanol (IPA, 1 mM), serving as sacrificial agents for superoxide radicals (·O2 -), holes (h+), and hydroxyl radicals (·OH), respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 6a, the photocatalytic efficiency was significantly diminished upon the addition of BQ and EDTA-2Na, thereby confirming the essential roles of h+ and ·O2 - in the photocatalytic process. In contrast, the introduction of IPA exhibited a limited effect on the photocatalytic activity, suggesting that only a limited number of ·OH radicals participated in the degradation of ciprofloxacin. To further investigate the involvement of ·OH radicals in this photocatalytic process, PL spectroscopy was performed utilizing terephthalic acid (TA) as a probe molecule. In this experiment, TA effectively captured hydroxyl radicals to yield highly fluorescent 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid, allowing for the measurement of fluorescence emission intensity at 425 nm. The observed increase in PL intensity with extended irradiation time, as demonstrated in Fig. 6b, indicates a minor generation of hydroxyl radicals on the photocatalyst's surface. These findings indicate that hydroxyl radicals do exist in the photoreaction media, despite their limited role, which aligns with the results obtained from the radical scavenger analysis.

In Fig. 7, we examine the potential charge-transfer mechanism and the associated photocatalytic processes of the GQDs@ZIS@HCOF heterostructure in the context of ciprofloxacin degradation, based on the characterization analyses and photocatalytic experiments conducted in this study. To facilitate this analysis, we employed XPS valence band techniques to ascertain the relative positions of the maximum valence band (VB) of ZnIn2S4 and HCOF. The valence band potentials for ZIS and HCOF, as illustrated in Figures S3 and S4 respectively, are determined to be 1.37 V and 1.75 V relative to the standard hydrogen electrode (NHE). The conduction band potentials (ECB) were computed using the empirical equation ECB = EVB - Eg. Based on the band gap values (Eg) detailed in Figure S1 and this equation, the ECB for pristine ZIS and HCOF is found to be −0.89 eV and −0.22 eV, respectively. An examination of the energy band arrangement depicted in Fig. 7 reveals that the conduction band edge of ZIS displays greater negativity compared to the ECB of HCOF. Additionally, it is significant to note that the VB position of HCOF is lower than that of ZIS. Under visible light exposure, electrons in the valence bands of both HCOF and ZIS can be excited to their respective conduction bands due to their relatively narrow band gaps. This excitation process leads to the generation of holes in the valence band. As a result, holes transfer from HCOF to ZIS, while electrons move in the opposite direction, from ZIS to HCOF. This mechanism promotes the spatial separation of photoexcited charge carriers. Moreover, due to their exceptional electron mobility and affinity, GQDs can serve as electron mediators, thereby enhancing the charge transfer process. The excess electrons may also be transferred to the GQDs, which further reduces the recombination of electron-hole pairs.

Accumulated electrons in the CB of semiconductors can capture O2 to produce · O2 -, which played an important role in the photodegradation process as the above experimental results. At the same time, the photogenerated holes in the VB of ZIS with sufficient potential can be directly used to oxidize the pollutant molecules. The limited presence of hydroxyl radicals can be attributed to the higher standard redox potential of ·OH/OH- (1.99 eV versus NHE) in comparison to the valence band edge (EVB) of HCOF and ZIS. Consequently, the few hydroxyl radicals detected in the system are likely a result of the reaction between superoxide anions ( · O2 -) and photoinduced electrons. This finding presents a contradiction to the outcomes observed in the active species capture experiment. Based on the aforementioned considerations, the mechanism for the photodegradation process of ciprofloxacin may be summarized as follows: GQDs@ZIS@HCOF + hν → GQDs@ZIS@HCOF (e- + h+)
H2O2 + e- → ·OH
h+ / · O2 -/·OH + ciprofloxacin → degradation products

The photostability of catalysts constitutes a fundamental characteristic in practical photocatalytic applications. To assess the cycling photo-stability of the GQDs@ZIS@HCOF composite, specimens employed in the photocatalytic process were collected and subjected to four sequential cycles for the degradation of ciprofloxacin. Following each cycle, the samples were centrifuged, rinsed with ethanol and ultrapure water, and subsequently dried under vacuum conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 8a, the photocatalytic activity of the developed photocatalyst exhibited no significant deactivation over the course of four cycles of repeated application, thus demonstrating its high stability and reliable performance in practical scenarios. Additionally, the crystallinity of the cyclic GQDs@ZIS@HCOF photocatalyst was found to be virtually indistinguishable from that of the fresh photocatalyst (Fig. 8b). Furthermore, upon examining the SEM image of GQD@ZIS@HCOF prior to and following four cycles of reuse, as illustrated in Fig. 8b, it is evident that the topography of the photocatalyst remains intact. These observations indicate a high level of structural and chemical stability of the catalyst during the photocatalytic reaction process.

In this study, we successfully designed and synthesized a novel ternary hierarchical photocatalyst (GQDs@ZIS@HCOF) with a hollow spherical structure via a hard-template approach combined with a hydrothermal process. The unique structural features, including a hollow porous architecture, high surface area, and strong interfacial interactions, significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity. The optimized photocatalyst, consisting of 3 wt% GQDs, demonstrated a significant photocatalytic degradation efficiency for ciprofloxacin, achieving a remarkable 90 % reduction. This efficiency surpasses that of both pure HCOF and ZnIn2S4 by factors of six and seven, respectively. The enhanced performance is attributed to efficient light harvesting, increased active sites, and improved charge separation facilitated by the synergistic interactions among the components. Furthermore, the photocatalyst demonstrated excellent stability over four consecutive cycles, indicating its potential for long-term operation. Given its high efficiency and stability, the GQDs@ZIS@HCOF composite holds promise for scalable applications in wastewater treatment and environmental remediation. This study may pave the way for innovative strategies in the synthesis of advanced nanostructures with controllable morphologies tailored for visible-light-driven photocatalytic functions.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Mohammadi Ziarani Ghodsi: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Banitalebi Ali: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Badiei Alireza: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Alzahra University and the University of Tehran for assisting this research financially.
